title: Listening - Part 4: Returning to Education
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five mature students discussing their reasons for returning to university and the challenges they faced

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been working in retail management for nearly two decades, and while I was good at it, I felt I’d hit a glass ceiling. [21]I realized that without a formal degree, I simply wasn't being considered for the high-level strategic roles I coveted[/21]. Returning to study economics at forty was daunting. The biggest hurdle wasn't the curriculum itself, but [26]the struggle to balance intensive library sessions with the demands of my three young children[/26]. There were many late nights where I was writing essays on my laptop while rocking a cradle with my foot. It was physically and mentally draining, but knowing I’m finally on the path to a better career keeps me going through the exhaustion. || Speaker 2: I originally left school at sixteen to join a band, and I never looked back until recently. [22]I decided to study history of art purely because I felt I’d missed out on a fundamental part of my intellectual development[/22]. I wanted to fill the gaps in my knowledge. It’s been a revelation, honestly. However, [27]I found the transition to the highly structured, academic style of writing incredibly restrictive and frustrating[/27] at first. I’m used to expressing myself creatively and loosely, so having to cite every single thought and follow rigid formatting felt like wearing a mental straightjacket. I’ve had to completely relearn how to think and communicate in a formal way, which was a blow to my ego. || Speaker 3: I spent my life in the military, and when I retired, I felt completely adrift. [23]I took up a psychology degree because I wanted to understand my own experiences and help other veterans transitioning to civilian life[/23]. It’s been a healing process for me. The academic side was actually quite manageable, but [28]I felt a profound sense of alienation being surrounded by nineteen-year-olds who had such a different worldview[/28]. They were worried about social media and parties, while I was reflecting on life-and-death situations. It took a long time to find common ground with my classmates, but once I did, I realized we actually had a lot to teach each other about resilience. || Speaker 4: My primary motivation was purely practical. [24]I’ve always been passionate about environmental science, and I wanted to gain the credentials to start my own consultancy firm[/24]. I didn't want to work for anyone else anymore. The course is brilliant, but [29]I was completely blindsided by the sheer complexity of the digital tools and software we’re expected to master[/29]. I’m from the generation of pen and paper, so navigating virtual learning environments and complex data modeling software was a massive learning curve. I spent the first semester just trying to figure out how to upload my assignments correctly, which was quite humbling for someone who’s used to being in charge. || Speaker 5: I’d spent years as a stay-at-home parent, and [25]I decided to study literature to regain a sense of identity that wasn't just 'Mum'[/25]. I needed something for myself. It’s been wonderful to engage with complex ideas again. The most rewarding part, however, was unexpected. [30]I’ve discovered a level of academic discipline and focus that I never possessed when I was younger[/30]. Back then, I was easily distracted, but now I’m much more efficient and dedicated. My professors have actually commented on my work ethic. It’s been a huge boost to my self-esteem to realize that I’m actually a much better student now than I was at eighteen. I’m finally finding my own voice.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main reason for returning to study.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions hitting a glass ceiling and needing a degree for high-level roles.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 felt he had missed out on fundamental intellectual development.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to understand his experiences and help fellow veterans.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted the credentials to start her own consultancy business.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to regain a sense of identity beyond being a parent.

options.A: to fulfill a lifelong academic ambition
options.B: to assist a specific social group
options.C: to address a perceived lack of education
options.D: to meet the requirements of a current employer
options.F: to establish an independent sense of self
options.G: to change their career path entirely
options.H: to facilitate the opening of a private business

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker found most challenging or surprising.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the struggle of balancing library sessions with the demands of young children.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found the rigid, academic style of writing to be restrictive and frustrating.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 felt alienated because of the age gap and different worldviews of his classmates.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 was surprised by the complexity of the digital tools and software.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 was surprised to find she had much better discipline and focus than when she was younger.

options.B: the financial burden of tuition fees
options.C: the difficulty of relating to younger peers
options.D: a lack of support from university staff
options.E: the physical toll of long hours of study
options.H: the discovery of improved learning habits
